cot_prefix: Okay, let me try to figure out this problem. It's about a teacher and four students who are given numbers from a set S. The set S has four consecutive two-digit integers. Also, some number in S is divisible by 6, and a different number is divisible by 7. The students all say they can't figure out what S is initially, but after hearing that, they can. I need to find the sum of all possible values of the greatest element of S. First, let me recap the problem to make sure I understand. The set S has four consecutive two-digit numbers. Each student gets one number from S, all different. The teacher tells them that in S, there's one divisible by 6 and another by 7. Then the teacher asks if they can figure out S. All four say no. Then, upon hearing that they all said no, they can figure it out. So I need to find all such sets S where this logic works, and sum their maximum elements. Okay, so step one: figure out all possible sets S of four consecutive two-digit integers where one is divisible by 6 and another by 7. Then, figure out which of those sets have the property that when each student is given a number, they all say "no," but then can deduce S after that. Since the students are perfectly logical, their initial "no" gives information. So maybe some sets can be eliminated based on that. First, let me list all possible sets S. Four consecutive two-digit integers. The numbers are from 10 to 99. So the sets S could be {n, n+1, n+2, n+3} where n is from 10 to 96 (since n+3 has to be ≤ 99).
